title: UTI — Complicated
oneLiner: UTI in patients with structural/functional abnormality, catheter, male sex, diabetes, or immunosuppression; high risk of pyelonephritis and sepsis.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Sepsis / septic shock:** fever >38.3°C, rigors, hypotension (systolic <90 mmHg), lactate >2 mmol/L, altered mental state → this is not a simple cystitis; start IV antibiotics and fluids immediately.
- **Obstructive pyelonephritis (pyonephrosis):** unilateral loin pain + high fever + rigors + vomiting; USS shows hydronephrosis and debris → this needs urgent urology review for decompression (nephrostomy or stent); delay can cause renal loss.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** dysuria, urinary frequency/urgency, suprapubic pain, loin pain (unilateral or bilateral), fevers/rigors, nausea/vomiting, confusion (elderly).
- **Key exam findings:** costovertebral angle (CVA) tenderness, suprapubic tenderness, tachycardia, fever, hypotension (if septic). Men – rectal exam to rule out prostatitis (tender, boggy prostate).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Cystitis (simple UTI)** – no systemic symptoms (afebrile, no flank pain), localized to lower urinary tract.
- **Pyelonephritis (uncomplicated)** – fever, flank pain, but no complicating factors (young, female, healthy).
- **Renal abscess / perinephric abscess** – persistent fever despite antibiotics, focal tenderness, may have history of stones or diabetes.
- **Prostatitis (acute bacterial)** – perineal pain, difficulty voiding, tender prostate on rectal exam; usually in men <50.
- **Urolithiasis without infection** – colicky pain, haematuria, no fever, no systemic signs.
- **Interstitial cystitis / bladder pain syndrome** – chronic pain, no fever, negative urine culture.

- **Splitter:** Presence of systemic inflammatory response (fever, raised CRP, leucocytosis) = complicated/pyelonephritis vs simple cystitis (afebrile, normal CRP).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** urine dipstick (nitrites, leucocytes, blood) – positive suggests UTI; pregnancy test in all women of childbearing age.
- **Bloods:** FBC (WCC, neutrophils), CRP, U&Es (renal function), lactate, blood cultures (x2 sets if febrile).
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** renal tract ultrasound (within 24-48 hours) – look for obstruction, abscess, stones, hydronephrosis.
  - **Second-line:** CT KUB (if USS inconclusive or high suspicion of stone/abscess, or if pyonephrosis suspected).
- **Special tests:** mid-stream urine culture and sensitivity (start before antibiotics if possible; if already started, note on lab form). Consider STI screen (chlamydia/gonorrhoea) in sexually active young patients with urethritis symptoms.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - Complicated UTI = UTI plus at least one of: male sex, catheter, pregnancy, diabetes, immunosuppression, renal impairment, structural abnormality (stones, stents, ileal conduit), recent instrumentation.
  - QSOFA (≥2: RR≥22, AMS, SBP≤100) – screen for sepsis.
- **Never-miss test:** Blood cultures (x2) before IV antibiotics in any febrile or septic patient. Also, formal urine culture before starting antibiotics (if possible) to guide targeted therapy.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess NEWS2 and lactate; if sepsis suspected (NEWS ≥5 or lactate >2), start sepsis six: oxygen, IV fluids, blood cultures, IV antibiotics, lactate, monitor urine output. Escalate to ICU if lactate >4 or hypotension refractory to fluids.
  - Start empirical IV antibiotics (see Drugs & Doses). Consider one dose of IV gentamicin if severely septic (but ensure renal function checked and fluid resuscitated).
  - If obstruction suspected (hydronephrosis + fever) – call urology for emergency decompression (nephrostomy/stent). Do not wait for imaging if clinical suspicion high.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue IV antibiotics until afebrile and improving clinically (typically 48-72h). Then step down to oral antibiotics based on sensitivities.
  - Monitor NEWS2, urinary output, renal function (U&Es daily while on IV gentamicin or if AKI).
  - If catheter present: remove or replace (new catheter after starting antibiotics; consider catheter specimen of urine for culture).
  - Imaging results: if stone or abscess found, discuss with urology/radiology re drainage.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete antibiotic course (7-14 days for complicated, 14-21 days for pyelonephritis with complicating factor).
  - Address underlying cause: remove catheter, treat stones (lithotripsy/surgery), manage diabetes, investigate for structural anomalies (CT urogram if recurrent).
  - Consider prophylaxis if frequent recurrences (post-menopausal women: vaginal oestrogen; others: low-dose antibiotic as per microbiology).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride 1L over 8h (adjust for renal function, heart failure). If septic, bolus 500 mL over 15 min, reassess.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** dalteparin 5000 units SC OD (or enoxaparin 40 mg) if not bleeding and eGFR >30; adjust for weight.
  - **Diet/NBM status:** allow diet unless vomiting or planned procedure (e.g., stent insertion).
  - **Observation frequency:** 4-hourly NEWS if stable; if sepsis, consider 1-hourly until improvement.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (IV):**
  - Co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV TDS (if no allergy and local sensitivity pattern supports).
  - Cefuroxime 750 mg IV TDS (alternative if penicillin allergic but not immediate hypersensitivity).
  - Gentamicin (single dose 5-7 mg/kg IV once daily) – only for severely septic or if beta-lactam allergy. **[check local policy for dosing and monitoring – requires renal function, levels, and audiology if prolonged]**
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Ciprofloxacin 400 mg IV BD (for beta-lactam allergy; avoid if known tendon issues or in pregnancy).
  - Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV TDS (if broad-spectrum needed, e.g., suspect ESBL or urosepsis in hospitalised patient).
- **Oral step-down (after culture:**
  - Trimethoprim 200 mg PO BD (if sensitive)
  - Nitrofurantoin 50-100 mg PO QDS OR MR 100 mg BD **(BUT NOT for pyelonephritis/complicated UTI – poor renal tissue penetration)**
  - Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO BD (if sensitive, but caution with interactions and tendon risk)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Nitrofurantoin: avoid if eGFR <45 mL/min, or in pyelonephritis/complicated UTI.
  - Ciprofloxacin: avoid in patients on theophylline (increases levels), warfarin (increases INR), and in children/pregnancy.
  - Gentamicin: avoid in myasthenia gravis; monitor levels (trough <1 mg/L for once-daily dosing); check baseline audiometry if prolonged course.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Co-amoxiclav: adjust if CrCl <30: usual dose 1.2 g, then 0.6 g daily (or as per BNF).
  - Gentamicin: typical once-daily dose, but urea and creatinine must be checked; levels required at 24h (trough). Adjust dose interval per local nomogram.
  - Ciprofloxacin: reduce dose if CrCl <30 (e.g., 250-500 mg PO daily, or 200 mg IV daily).
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - Gentamicin — giving a second dose without checking trough level (risk of nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity). Always check levels before second dose in renal impairment.
  - Co-amoxiclav — prescribing without checking penicillin allergy history (risk of anaphylaxis in true allergy).
  - Ciprofloxacin — prescribing in elderly with tenofovir or theophylline without reducing dose (risk of seizures or arrhythmia).
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Sepsis / septic shock
  - Acute kidney injury (AKI)
  - Emphysematous pyelonephritis (gas in renal parenchyma – surgical emergency)
  - Renal / perinephric abscess (needs drainage)
  - Urosepsis with DIC
  - Acute papillary necrosis (especially in diabetics)
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrent pyelonephritis / chronic pyelonephritis → renal scarring → hypertension, chronic kidney disease
  - Renal stone formation (if infection with urease-producing organisms like Proteus)
  - Catheter lithiasis and encrustation
sections.Prognosis: - Mortality <5% for uncomplicated complicated UTI, but rises to 20-30% if septic shock or emphysematous pyelonephritis. Most patients recover fully with prompt antibiotics and drainage. Recurrence rate high if underlying factor not addressed (e.g., catheter, stone).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always send pre-treatment urine culture** (even if empirical antibiotics started immediately) – helps de-escalate and guide resistance.
- **Do a renal USS within 24 hours** in all male patients with UTI, all with recurrent pyelonephritis, and in any case of complicated UTI with fever >48h despite antibiotics.
- **Remove/replace the catheter** in catheter-associated UTI – this is the single most effective step in management; do not treat through an old catheter.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not use nitrofurantoin for pyelonephritis/complicated UTI** – it fails to achieve adequate renal tissue levels.
- **Do not discharge a patient with complicated UTI while still febrile** or with significant flank pain – risk of progression to abscess/sepsis.
- **Do not forget to check for obstruction** in a patient with pyelonephritis who fails to improve after 48h on appropriate antibiotics – USS and urology consult are mandatory.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** fever returns or persists >48h on oral antibiotics; worsening loin pain; vomiting preventing oral intake; new confusion or difficulty passing urine.
- **Follow-up:** Urine culture results in 48-72h – review by GP or ward nurse practitioner; if culture shows resistance, change antibiotics. Repeat urine culture after treatment (test of cure) only if recurrent symptoms or pregnancy.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / urology / ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L (persistent after fluid bolus)
  - Systolic BP <90 mmHg after 1L fluid
  - GCS <15 or new confusion
  - Anuria or rising creatinine despite adequate hydration
  - Known obstruction or hydronephrosis on USS (urgent urology for decompression)
- **Call crash team (2222) if:** NEWS2 ≥7, or any of: airway compromise, no pulse, unresponsive.

rawMarkdown:
## UTI — Complicated

**One-liner:** UTI in patients with structural/functional abnormality, catheter, male sex, diabetes, or immunosuppression; high risk of pyelonephritis and sepsis.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Sepsis / septic shock:** fever >38.3°C, rigors, hypotension (systolic <90 mmHg), lactate >2 mmol/L, altered mental state → this is not a simple cystitis; start IV antibiotics and fluids immediately.
- **Obstructive pyelonephritis (pyonephrosis):** unilateral loin pain + high fever + rigors + vomiting; USS shows hydronephrosis and debris → this needs urgent urology review for decompression (nephrostomy or stent); delay can cause renal loss.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** dysuria, urinary frequency/urgency, suprapubic pain, loin pain (unilateral or bilateral), fevers/rigors, nausea/vomiting, confusion (elderly).
- **Key exam findings:** costovertebral angle (CVA) tenderness, suprapubic tenderness, tachycardia, fever, hypotension (if septic). Men – rectal exam to rule out prostatitis (tender, boggy prostate).

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Cystitis (simple UTI)** – no systemic symptoms (afebrile, no flank pain), localized to lower urinary tract.
- **Pyelonephritis (uncomplicated)** – fever, flank pain, but no complicating factors (young, female, healthy).
- **Renal abscess / perinephric abscess** – persistent fever despite antibiotics, focal tenderness, may have history of stones or diabetes.
- **Prostatitis (acute bacterial)** – perineal pain, difficulty voiding, tender prostate on rectal exam; usually in men <50.
- **Urolithiasis without infection** – colicky pain, haematuria, no fever, no systemic signs.
- **Interstitial cystitis / bladder pain syndrome** – chronic pain, no fever, negative urine culture.

- **Splitter:** Presence of systemic inflammatory response (fever, raised CRP, leucocytosis) = complicated/pyelonephritis vs simple cystitis (afebrile, normal CRP).

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** urine dipstick (nitrites, leucocytes, blood) – positive suggests UTI; pregnancy test in all women of childbearing age.
- **Bloods:** FBC (WCC, neutrophils), CRP, U&Es (renal function), lactate, blood cultures (x2 sets if febrile).
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** renal tract ultrasound (within 24-48 hours) – look for obstruction, abscess, stones, hydronephrosis.
  - **Second-line:** CT KUB (if USS inconclusive or high suspicion of stone/abscess, or if pyonephrosis suspected).
- **Special tests:** mid-stream urine culture and sensitivity (start before antibiotics if possible; if already started, note on lab form). Consider STI screen (chlamydia/gonorrhoea) in sexually active young patients with urethritis symptoms.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - Complicated UTI = UTI plus at least one of: male sex, catheter, pregnancy, diabetes, immunosuppression, renal impairment, structural abnormality (stones, stents, ileal conduit), recent instrumentation.
  - QSOFA (≥2: RR≥22, AMS, SBP≤100) – screen for sepsis.
- **Never-miss test:** Blood cultures (x2) before IV antibiotics in any febrile or septic patient. Also, formal urine culture before starting antibiotics (if possible) to guide targeted therapy.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess NEWS2 and lactate; if sepsis suspected (NEWS ≥5 or lactate >2), start sepsis six: oxygen, IV fluids, blood cultures, IV antibiotics, lactate, monitor urine output. Escalate to ICU if lactate >4 or hypotension refractory to fluids.
  - Start empirical IV antibiotics (see Drugs & Doses). Consider one dose of IV gentamicin if severely septic (but ensure renal function checked and fluid resuscitated).
  - If obstruction suspected (hydronephrosis + fever) – call urology for emergency decompression (nephrostomy/stent). Do not wait for imaging if clinical suspicion high.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue IV antibiotics until afebrile and improving clinically (typically 48-72h). Then step down to oral antibiotics based on sensitivities.
  - Monitor NEWS2, urinary output, renal function (U&Es daily while on IV gentamicin or if AKI).
  - If catheter present: remove or replace (new catheter after starting antibiotics; consider catheter specimen of urine for culture).
  - Imaging results: if stone or abscess found, discuss with urology/radiology re drainage.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete antibiotic course (7-14 days for complicated, 14-21 days for pyelonephritis with complicating factor).
  - Address underlying cause: remove catheter, treat stones (lithotripsy/surgery), manage diabetes, investigate for structural anomalies (CT urogram if recurrent).
  - Consider prophylaxis if frequent recurrences (post-menopausal women: vaginal oestrogen; others: low-dose antibiotic as per microbiology).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride 1L over 8h (adjust for renal function, heart failure). If septic, bolus 500 mL over 15 min, reassess.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** dalteparin 5000 units SC OD (or enoxaparin 40 mg) if not bleeding and eGFR >30; adjust for weight.
  - **Diet/NBM status:** allow diet unless vomiting or planned procedure (e.g., stent insertion).
  - **Observation frequency:** 4-hourly NEWS if stable; if sepsis, consider 1-hourly until improvement.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents (IV):**
  - Co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV TDS (if no allergy and local sensitivity pattern supports).
  - Cefuroxime 750 mg IV TDS (alternative if penicillin allergic but not immediate hypersensitivity).
  - Gentamicin (single dose 5-7 mg/kg IV once daily) – only for severely septic or if beta-lactam allergy. **[check local policy for dosing and monitoring – requires renal function, levels, and audiology if prolonged]**
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Ciprofloxacin 400 mg IV BD (for beta-lactam allergy; avoid if known tendon issues or in pregnancy).
  - Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV TDS (if broad-spectrum needed, e.g., suspect ESBL or urosepsis in hospitalised patient).
- **Oral step-down (after culture:**
  - Trimethoprim 200 mg PO BD (if sensitive)
  - Nitrofurantoin 50-100 mg PO QDS OR MR 100 mg BD **(BUT NOT for pyelonephritis/complicated UTI – poor renal tissue penetration)**
  - Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO BD (if sensitive, but caution with interactions and tendon risk)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Nitrofurantoin: avoid if eGFR <45 mL/min, or in pyelonephritis/complicated UTI.
  - Ciprofloxacin: avoid in patients on theophylline (increases levels), warfarin (increases INR), and in children/pregnancy.
  - Gentamicin: avoid in myasthenia gravis; monitor levels (trough <1 mg/L for once-daily dosing); check baseline audiometry if prolonged course.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Co-amoxiclav: adjust if CrCl <30: usual dose 1.2 g, then 0.6 g daily (or as per BNF).
  - Gentamicin: typical once-daily dose, but urea and creatinine must be checked; levels required at 24h (trough). Adjust dose interval per local nomogram.
  - Ciprofloxacin: reduce dose if CrCl <30 (e.g., 250-500 mg PO daily, or 200 mg IV daily).
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - Gentamicin — giving a second dose without checking trough level (risk of nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity). Always check levels before second dose in renal impairment.
  - Co-amoxiclav — prescribing without checking penicillin allergy history (risk of anaphylaxis in true allergy).
  - Ciprofloxacin — prescribing in elderly with tenofovir or theophylline without reducing dose (risk of seizures or arrhythmia).

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Sepsis / septic shock
  - Acute kidney injury (AKI)
  - Emphysematous pyelonephritis (gas in renal parenchyma – surgical emergency)
  - Renal / perinephric abscess (needs drainage)
  - Urosepsis with DIC
  - Acute papillary necrosis (especially in diabetics)
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrent pyelonephritis / chronic pyelonephritis → renal scarring → hypertension, chronic kidney disease
  - Renal stone formation (if infection with urease-producing organisms like Proteus)
  - Catheter lithiasis and encrustation

### Prognosis
- Mortality <5% for uncomplicated complicated UTI, but rises to 20-30% if septic shock or emphysematous pyelonephritis. Most patients recover fully with prompt antibiotics and drainage. Recurrence rate high if underlying factor not addressed (e.g., catheter, stone).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always send pre-treatment urine culture** (even if empirical antibiotics started immediately) – helps de-escalate and guide resistance.
- **Do a renal USS within 24 hours** in all male patients with UTI, all with recurrent pyelonephritis, and in any case of complicated UTI with fever >48h despite antibiotics.
- **Remove/replace the catheter** in catheter-associated UTI – this is the single most effective step in management; do not treat through an old catheter.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not use nitrofurantoin for pyelonephritis/complicated UTI** – it fails to achieve adequate renal tissue levels.
- **Do not discharge a patient with complicated UTI while still febrile** or with significant flank pain – risk of progression to abscess/sepsis.
- **Do not forget to check for obstruction** in a patient with pyelonephritis who fails to improve after 48h on appropriate antibiotics – USS and urology consult are mandatory.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** fever returns or persists >48h on oral antibiotics; worsening loin pain; vomiting preventing oral intake; new confusion or difficulty passing urine.
- **Follow-up:** Urine culture results in 48-72h – review by GP or ward nurse practitioner; if culture shows resistance, change antibiotics. Repeat urine culture after treatment (test of cure) only if recurrent symptoms or pregnancy.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / urology / ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L (persistent after fluid bolus)
  - Systolic BP <90 mmHg after 1L fluid
  - GCS <15 or new confusion
  - Anuria or rising creatinine despite adequate hydration
  - Known obstruction or hydronephrosis on USS (urgent urology for decompression)
- **Call crash team (2222) if:** NEWS2 ≥7, or any of: airway compromise, no pulse, unresponsive.
